title: The Purple Coast
bullets: The Phoenician homeland was a 200-mile strip of coast pinned between the Mediterranean and the Lebanon Mountains, roughly the size of half a Rhode Island, containing the three greatest natural harbors in the ancient world.

Byblos, Sidon, and Tyre were independent city-states that never unified into a single nation, and that political fragmentation was both their permanent weakness against empires and their survival advantage when any one city fell.
Egyptian records from 2600 BCE describe buying cedar from Byblos, making the Byblos-Egypt trade route one of the oldest documented commercial relationships in human history.
Byblos re-exported Egyptian papyrus so thoroughly that the Greeks named their word for book after the city: biblos became biblion, which became Bible.

Rib-Hadda of Byblos wrote over sixty letters to Pharaoh Akhenaten in the Amarna archive, the most prolific correspondent in the collection, tracing a slow-motion political crisis as Egyptian support evaporated.
Ugarit, 100 miles north of Byblos, developed a cuneiform alphabet of 30 letters around 1300 BCE and produced the Baal Cycle, the most complete surviving example of Canaanite-Phoenician mythology.
The Phoenicians called themselves Tyrians, Sidonians, or Byblians; the name Phoenician comes from the Greek phoinikes, meaning purple people, a reference to the famous dye they produced.
Ugarit was destroyed around 1185 BCE; one of the last tablets found in the ruins was a letter pleading for help, found still in the kiln, unbaked, never sent.

title: Children of the Collapse
bullets: The Bronze Age Collapse destroyed the Hittite Empire, ended Mycenaean Greece, forced Egypt out of the Levant, and crippled Assyria, but Byblos, Sidon, and Tyre all came through battered but standing.

With the Hittites gone, Egypt retreated, and Mycenaean Greece in ruins, the Phoenician cities found themselves the dominant maritime traders of the entire Mediterranean for the first time in their history.

Phoenician shipwrights developed the keeled hull for open-water stability, mortise-and-tenon joinery for watertight planking, and the bireme for doubled rowing power, making their ships the most advanced vessels in the world.
Tyre emerged as the dominant Phoenician city after the collapse, partly because its island location half a mile offshore made it virtually impervious to land-based military assault.

Gadir (modern Cadiz) was founded on the Atlantic coast of Spain beyond the Strait of Gibraltar, putting the Phoenicians in contact with Tartessos and some of the richest silver deposits in the ancient world.

Phoenician navigators sailed by Ursa Minor rather than the less accurate Ursa Major used by other Mediterranean sailors, and the Greeks were so impressed they called the Little Bear the Phoenician constellation.

By 1000 BCE the Phoenicians had the Mediterranean to themselves: the Zhou ruled inland China, Vedic India was centered on rivers, Egypt had retreated from the sea, and Greece was in its post-Mycenaean dark age.

title: The Alphabet
bullets: The Phoenician alphabet consisted of 22 letters, each representing a single consonant sound, a system so simple a merchant could learn it in a week compared to the 600-1,000 signs of Mesopotamian cuneiform.

The alphabet's ancestor was Proto-Sinaitic, scratched by Semitic miners in the Sinai around 1800-1500 BCE using simplified Egyptian hieroglyphs: a picture of an ox head (aleph) became the letter A, which is still an upside-down ox head.

The Ahiram Sarcophagus from Byblos carries one of the oldest substantial inscriptions in the mature Phoenician script, a curse warning that any king who disturbs the coffin will have his throne overturned.
The Phoenician script was an abjad recording only consonants, which works tolerably for Semitic languages but became an obstacle when the Greeks adopted it and needed vowels.

The Greeks repurposed Phoenician consonant letters for sounds absent in Greek: aleph became alpha (A), he became epsilon (E), ayin became omicron (O), yod became iota (I), and waw became upsilon (U), creating the first true alphabet.
The Greeks called their letters phoinikeia grammata (Phoenician letters) and credited the mythological Cadmus, explicitly identified as a Phoenician prince, with bringing the alphabet to Greece.
Through Greek, Etruscan, and Latin transmission, plus separate lines to Aramaic, Hebrew, Arabic, and possibly Brahmi, virtually every alphabet on earth descends from the Phoenician script.

Sanchuniathon of Berytus reportedly wrote Phoenician histories and cosmogonies around 1100 BCE, but the entire Phoenician literary tradition was written on papyrus and parchment, which rot, and has been almost completely lost.

title: Hiram's Golden Age
bullets: Hiram I of Tyre (ruled approximately 980-947 BCE) built a commercial network so profitable that it made Tyre indispensable to every major power in the region during a window when no great empire controlled the Levant.

Hiram supplied Solomon with cedar, craftsmen, and luxury goods for the First Temple in Jerusalem; Solomon gave Hiram access to Red Sea trade routes and possibly a joint fleet at Ezion-Geber on the Gulf of Aqaba.

The Temple of Melqart at Tyre featured an annual ritual called the egersis in which the god was ritually killed and resurrected each spring, mirroring the Mesopotamian Tammuz, the Egyptian Osiris, and the Byblian Adonis.
Tyrian purple was extracted from murex sea snails: each snail yielded a single drop, roughly 10,000 snails produced one gram of dye, and a pound of purple-dyed wool cost as much as a pound of silver.

Massive deposits of crushed murex shells, some tens of feet deep, have been found at Sidon, and ancient writers consistently describe the stench of the extraction process as one of the worst things about Phoenician coastal cities.
Ithobaal I, a priest of Astarte, seized the throne of Tyre around 887 BCE and united Tyre and Sidon under a single ruler for the first time, then married his daughter Jezebel to King Ahab of Israel.

Phoenician ivory carvings blended Egyptian sphinxes, Mesopotamian sacred trees, and Levantine geometric borders into a distinctive hybrid style, and thousands of them have been excavated from the Assyrian palaces at Nimrud.
In 842 BCE, Shalmaneser III of Assyria forced Tyre and Sidon to pay heavy tribute, ending the golden age of Phoenician independence.

title: The Great Colonizers
bullets: Between 1100 and 600 BCE, Phoenician traders established permanent settlements in a 3,000-mile arc from the Levant to the Atlantic coast of Spain and Morocco, one trading post at a time, without conquering anyone.
Carthage was founded in 814 BCE by the Tyrian princess Dido (Elissa), who fled after her brother Pygmalion murdered her husband Acerbas, the high priest of Melqart, for his treasure.

Carthage sat on a peninsula commanding the narrow strait between Tunisia and Sicily, the chokepoint between the eastern and western Mediterranean, and within two centuries surpassed its mother city Tyre.

The Nora Stone from Sardinia is the oldest known Phoenician inscription found outside the Levant, probably from the ninth or eighth century BCE, and contains the earliest written reference to Sardinia in any language.

Phoenician merchants at Gadir traded with Tartessos, tapping the Guadalquivir valley's silver deposits at sites like Rio Tinto, which has been mined almost continuously from Phoenician times to the present day.

Around 600 BCE, Phoenician sailors commissioned by Pharaoh Necho II reportedly circumnavigated Africa, a feat not repeated for over two thousand years; the detail Herodotus disbelieved (the sun appearing to the north) is actually the strongest evidence the voyage happened.

Around 500 BCE, the Carthaginian admiral Hanno sailed down the West African coast, observed creatures his interpreters called Gorillae (probably great apes), and when scientists formally described the gorilla in 1847 they named it after his account.

The Phoenician colonial network was not an empire but a web of autonomous trading posts connected by shared language, the cult of Melqart, and informal merchant-family networks spanning the entire Mediterranean.

title: Under the Empires
bullets: The Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III, now in the British Museum, shows Phoenician kings alongside other Levantine rulers prostrating themselves before the Assyrian king and presenting tribute of silver, gold, textiles, and ivory.

Luli of Tyre refused to submit to Sennacherib in 701 BCE and fled to Cyprus; the Assyrians besieged Tyre's island fortress but could not take it without naval superiority and eventually settled for a puppet king on the mainland.

Nebuchadnezzar II besieged Tyre for thirteen years starting in 586 BCE, more than eighteen times longer than Alexander's famous seven-month siege, and still did not capture the island in the traditional sense.
Ezekiel 27 lists Tyre's trading partners in a commercial gazetteer: silver from Tartessos, slaves from Greece, horses from Anatolia, wheat from Israel, wine from Damascus, and lambs from Arabia.

Under Persian rule the Phoenician cities kept their kings and commerce, and Phoenician ships formed the elite core of the Persian navy; at Salamis in 480 BCE, Xerxes reportedly punished his Phoenician captains for the defeat while praising others.

Eshmunazar II of Sidon built the Temple of Eshmun, a grand healing sanctuary with monumental terraces and ritual baths near the Awali River, one of the best-preserved pieces of Phoenician monumental architecture outside Carthage.

In 351 BCE, Tennes of Sidon revolted against Persia with Egyptian backing, then betrayed his own city to save himself; Artaxerxes III burned Sidon to the ground, reportedly killing 40,000, and executed Tennes anyway.

The Phoenicians survived five centuries of imperial domination not because they were strong but because they were useful: every conqueror discovered that the cities were more valuable intact than destroyed.

title: The Carthaginian Century
bullets: Mago I (ruled approximately 550-530 BCE) professionalized the Carthaginian army, forged an alliance with the Etruscans, and at the Battle of Alalia around 535 BCE divided the western Mediterranean into Carthaginian and Etruscan spheres of influence.

At the Battle of Himera in 480 BCE, the same year as Salamis, Gelon of Syracuse destroyed a Carthaginian invasion force and killed Hamilcar I, who reportedly threw himself into the sacrificial fire when the day was lost.

The Cothon, Carthage's double harbor, hid a circular military harbor behind the commercial port, sheltering 220 warships in individual covered slips around a central admiralty island, invisible from the sea.

The Tophet of Carthage contains thousands of urns with cremated infant remains, but scholars are genuinely split on whether they represent ritual child sacrifice or special burial of infants who died of natural causes.

Rome had no navy at the start of the First Punic War (264-241 BCE) but built one from scratch, adding the corvus boarding bridge that turned naval battles into land battles and negated Carthaginian seamanship.

Hannibal Barca marched 50,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants from Spain across the Alps in late autumn of 218 BCE, losing roughly half his army to rockslides, hostile tribes, and freezing temperatures before fighting a single battle.

At Cannae in 216 BCE, Hannibal's double envelopment destroyed a Roman army of roughly 80,000 men, killing between 50,000 and 70,000 in a single day, the worst military disaster in Roman history and still military shorthand for a battle of annihilation.

Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal at Zama in 202 BCE by turning Hannibal's own signature double envelopment against him with Numidian cavalry; Carthage surrendered its fleet, its territories, and 10,000 talents of silver.

Hannibal killed himself by poison in exile in 183 BCE, reportedly saying, 'Let us relieve the Romans from the anxiety they have so long experienced, since they think it tries their patience too much to wait for an old man's death.'

title: The End of Phoenicia
bullets: Alexander the Great built a causeway half a mile across open water to reach Tyre's island fortress, demolishing the abandoned mainland city for rubble and fighting off Tyrian fire-ships, divers, and catapult fire for seven months.

After the breach in August 332 BCE, Alexander's troops killed 8,000 Tyrians, crucified 2,000 military-age men along the beach, and sold 30,000 civilians into slavery; the causeway silted up and permanently turned the island into a peninsula.

Cato the Elder reportedly ended every speech in the Roman Senate, regardless of topic, with Carthago delenda est (Carthage must be destroyed), capturing Rome's pathological anxiety about the city that had nearly destroyed them.

During the three-year siege of the Third Punic War (149-146 BCE), the disarmed Carthaginians forged new weapons from household metal, women braided their hair into bowstrings, and the population fought from walls, rooftops, and streets.

Scipio Aemilianus, the adopted grandson of the victor of Zama, broke through the walls in 146 BCE and fought street by street for six days; the city burned for seventeen days and 50,000 survivors were sold into slavery.

The legend that Rome plowed Carthage's ruins and sowed them with salt is probably apocryphal (first attested in the 19th century), but Rome did intend to erase the city from the map, and the same year it also destroyed Corinth.

Baalbek in Lebanon's Beqaa Valley, originally a Phoenician sacred precinct dedicated to Baal, survived into the Roman era as Heliopolis, where the Temple of Jupiter contains the Trilithon: three stones each weighing over 800 tons.

The Phoenicians left no empire and no grand monuments, but every alphabet from Iceland to Indonesia descends from their 22 consonant signs, and the cities they founded (Cadiz, Palermo, Tunis, Malaga) are still inhabited today.
